narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and LSAT score of 31. The admissions model evaluated your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, predicting an 82.3% chance that you would fail, which directly led to the rejection of your application since the school prioritizes students expected to succeed. This prediction hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which placed you in the bottom 20% of your peers and significantly pushed the model toward forecasting bar failure by signaling weaker academic performance later in your studies. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 2—again in the lower ranks compared to the typical student's midpoint around 5 or 6—which reinforced concerns about your early grades not building a strong foundation. Your undergraduate GPA offered a slight counterbalance, as it nudged the prediction somewhat toward passing, though not enough to offset the other red flags given that it's below the average of 3.21. These three academic indicators were the top drivers, far outweighing factors like your family income or full-time status, which aligns with the experiences of most applicants where grades in deciles this low often correlate with bar struggles. Compared to typical students who pass, your deciles are notably lower, while your LSAT trails the average of 36.7, painting a picture of consistent underperformance relative to peers. It's understandable to feel frustrated, especially as a full-time aspiring lawyer from a supportive background, but this model's view is data-driven from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve your decile rankings, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these core areas could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at schools that value growth stories like yours. Many students in similar positions have turned things around with targeted preparation—your next steps can rewrite this narrative.
